{"parentUuid":null,"isSidechain":true,"promptId":"1cc8c58e-5118-404c-b220-d25731c48877","agentId":"ae4c2f7fdb9cf91c2","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":"You are a historical research assistant. Do heavy web research on ALL major territorial/border changes from 1991 to present day (2025). This is for an academic project tracking \"polities\" (political entities defined by territory over time).\n\nFor each event, provide: exact year, territories involved, nature of change, area affected (km²) if possible, and resulting new polities.\n\nCover EXHAUSTIVELY:\n1. **Yugoslavia breakup (1991-2008)**: Slovenia 1991, Croatia 1991-1995 (including Krajina), Bosnia 1992-1995, Macedonia 1991, Federal Republic of Yugoslavia (Serbia+Montenegro) 1992, Kosovo War 1998-99, Serbia-Montenegro split 2006, Kosovo independence 2008\n2. **Czechoslovakia dissolution 1993**: Czech Republic and Slovakia\n3. **Eritrea independence from Ethiopia 1993**\n4. **German reunification 1990**\n5. **Yemen unification 1990**\n6. **Timor-Leste/East Timor independence 2002**\n7. **South Sudan independence 2011**\n8. **Crimea annexation by Russia 2014** (area: ~27,000 km²)\n9. **Russia-Ukraine war territorial changes 2022-present**: Donetsk, Luhansk, Zaporizhzhia, Kherson claimed by Russia\n10. **Other recent changes**: Any border agreements, territorial swaps, or new recognitions post-1991\n11. **Western Sahara status**: Morocco vs SADR\n12. **Hong Kong handover 1997, Macau handover 1999** (status changes)\n13. **Scotland referendum 2014, Brexit 2016** (did these create territorial changes? No, but note)\n14. **Bougainville autonomy referendum 2019**\n15. **New Caledonia referendums 2018-2021**\n16. **Any other territorial disputes or changes through 2025**\n\nAlso research and list any DATABASE SOURCES that track historical polities and borders:\n- Correlates of War (COW) project\n- CShapes dataset\n- Cliopatria\n- GADM\n- Natural Earth\n- Any others\n\nFor each change give the EXACT year and territories. Be exhaustive."},"uuid":"0f716829-7948-4c1b-aa6d-00b5b6a79ca8","timestamp":"2026-03-24T20:15:27.017Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"0f716829-7948-4c1b-aa6d-00b5b6a79ca8","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"ae4c2f7fdb9cf91c2","message":{"model":"claude-opus-4-6","id":"msg_01FxXiRpp4214HCejm5GE4ey","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"\n\n# Comprehensive Survey of Major Territorial/Border Changes, 1991–2025\n\nBelow is an exhaustive, year-by-year accounting of every significant territorial or sovereignty change since 1991, followed by a review of the major datasets that track historical polities and borders.\n\n---\n\n## 1. GERMAN REUNIFICATION (1990)\n\n- **Year:** 1990 (3 October)\n- **Nature:** The German Democratic Republic (East Germany) acceded to the Federal Republic of Germany (West Germany) under Article 23 of the Basic Law.\n- **Territories:** The five reconstituted Laender (Brandenburg, Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, Saxony, Saxony-Anhalt, Thuringia) plus East Berlin merged into the FRG.\n- **Area affected:** ~108,333 km² (area of the former GDR)\n- **Resulting polity:** Unified Federal Republic of Germany (~357,022 km²). The GDR ceased to exist as a sovereign state.\n\n## 2. YEMEN UNIFICATION (1990)\n\n- **Year:** 1990 (22 May)\n- **Nature:** The Yemen Arab Republic (North Yemen) and the People's Democratic Republic of Yemen (South Yemen) merged.\n- **Area:** North Yemen ~195,000 km²; South Yemen ~333,000 km². Combined Republic of Yemen ~528,000 km².\n- **Note:** A brief civil war in 1994 saw a failed attempt by the south to re-secede. Yemen remains nominally unified, though from 2014 onward the Houthi movement controls much of the north and a Southern Transitional Council controls parts of the south, creating de facto fragmentation (no internationally recognized border change).\n\n## 3. SOVIET UNION DISSOLUTION (1991)\n\n- **Year:** 1991 (declarations of independence mostly June–December 1991; USSR formally dissolved 26 December 1991)\n- **Nature:** The USSR fragmented into 15 sovereign successor states.\n- **Resulting polities and approximate areas:**\n\n| New State | Independence declared | Area (km²) |\n|---|---|---|\n| Lithuania | 1990-03-11 (recognized 1991) | 65,300 |\n| Georgia | 1991-04-09 | 69,700 |\n| Estonia | 1991-08-20 | 45,228 |\n| Latvia | 1991-08-21 | 64,589 |\n| Ukraine | 1991-08-24 | 603,550 |\n| Belarus | 1991-08-25 | 207,600 |\n| Moldova | 1991-08-27 | 33,846 |\n| Azerbaijan | 1991-08-30 | 86,600 |\n| Kyrgyzstan | 1991-08-31 | 199,951 |\n| Uzbekistan | 1991-09-01 | 448,978 |\n| Tajikistan | 1991-09-09 | 143,100 |\n| Armenia | 1991-09-21 | 29,743 |\n| Turkmenistan | 1991-10-27 | 488,100 |\n| Kazakhstan | 1991-12-16 | 2,724,900 |\n| Russian Federation | 1991-12-25 (successor) | 17,098,242 |\n\n- **De facto separatist entities arising from the dissolution:**\n  - **Transnistria** (from Moldova, 1990–92): ~4,163 km². Unrecognized.\n  - **South Ossetia** (from Georgia, 1991–92): ~3,900 km². Recognized by Russia (2008) and a handful of states.\n  - **Abkhazia** (from Georgia, 1992–93): ~8,660 km². Recognized by Russia (2008) and a handful of states.\n  - **Nagorno-Karabakh / \"Republic of Artsakh\"** (from Azerbaijan, 1991–94): ~11,458 km² at maximum extent (including surrounding occupied districts). Collapsed in September 2023 (see below).\n  - **Chechnya** (declared independence 1991; Russian military campaigns 1994–96, 1999–2009): Reincorporated into the Russian Federation; no lasting sovereignty change.\n\n## 4. YUGOSLAV WARS AND BREAKUP (1991–2008)\n\n### 4a. Slovenia (1991)\n- **Year:** 1991 (25 June declaration; Ten-Day War; recognized January 1992)\n- **Area:** ~20,273 km²\n- **Nature:** Secession from SFR Yugoslavia. Relatively clean break with minimal border disputes.\n\n### 4b. Croatia (1991–1998)\n- **Year:** 1991 (25 June declaration; recognized January 1992)\n- **Area:** ~56,594 km²\n- **Key sub-events:**\n  - **Republic of Serbian Krajina (RSK):** Serb-controlled breakaway within Croatia, ~17,000 km² at peak. Existed 1991–1995.\n  - **Operation Storm (August 1995):** Croatia recaptured most of the Krajina.\n  - **Eastern Slavonia (UNTAES):** The last Serb-held area around Vukovar was peacefully reintegrated into Croatia in 1998 under UN administration.\n- **Result:** Croatia with its full pre-war borders by 1998.\n\n### 4c. Bosnia and Herzegovina (1992–1995)\n- **Year:** 1992 (1 March referendum; recognized April 1992)\n- **Area:** ~51,197 km²\n- **Nature:** Three-sided war among Bosniaks, Croat, and Serb forces. The Dayton Agreement (December 1995) preserved Bosnia as a single sovereign state but divided internally into:\n  - **Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina** (Bosniak-Croat): ~26,110 km² (~51%)\n  - **Republika Srpska** (Bosnian Serb): ~24,857 km² (~49%)\n  - **Brcko District:** ~493 km² (joint condominium, formalized 2000)\n- **International borders unchanged;** internal entity boundaries established.\n\n### 4d. North Macedonia (1991)\n- **Year:** 1991 (8 September referendum; recognized 1993 as \"Former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia\"; renamed \"North Macedonia\" by the Prespa Agreement, 2019)\n- **Area:** ~25,713 km²\n- **Nature:** Peaceful secession. No territorial dispute with neighbors resolved until the name change (Greece had blocked NATO/EU membership).\n\n### 4e. Federal Republic of Yugoslavia / Serbia and Montenegro (1992–2006)\n- **Year:** 1992 (27 April) — Serbia and Montenegro proclaimed the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia (FRY).\n- **Renamed:** State Union of Serbia and Montenegro in 2003.\n- **Split:** Montenegro declared independence on 3 June 2006 after a referendum (21 May 2006, 55.5% for independence).\n  - **Montenegro area:** ~13,812 km²\n  - **Serbia area (without Kosovo):** ~77,474 km²\n\n### 4f. Kosovo (1998–2008)\n- **Kosovo War:** 1998–1999. NATO bombing campaign March–June 1999. UN Security Council Resolution 1244 placed Kosovo under UN administration (UNMIK) from June 1999.\n- **Independence declaration:** 17 February 2008.\n- **Area:** ~10,887 km²\n- **Recognition:** As of 2025, recognized by ~100 UN member states (including US, most EU members). Not recognized by Russia, China, Serbia, Spain, and others. Not a UN member state.\n\n## 5. CZECHOSLOVAKIA DISSOLUTION (1993)\n\n- **Year:** 1993 (1 January) — the \"Velvet Divorce\"\n- **Nature:** Peaceful negotiated dissolution of the Czech and Slovak Federative Republic.\n- **Czech Republic:** ~78,866 km²\n- **Slovakia:** ~49,035 km²\n- **No territorial disputes** resulted.\n\n## 6. ERITREA INDEPENDENCE FROM ETHIOPIA (1993)\n\n- **Year:** 1993 (24 May; referendum 23–25 April 1993, 99.8% in favor)\n- **Nature:** Eritrea had fought a 30-year war of independence (1961–1991). The EPLF captured Asmara in 1991; the referendum formalized independence.\n- **Area:** ~117,600 km²\n- **Subsequent conflict:** The Eritrea-Ethiopia border war (1998–2000) over Badme and other areas. The Eritrea-Ethiopia Boundary Commission (EEBC) issued a binding ruling in 2002 awarding Badme to Eritrea, but Ethiopia refused to implement it until the 2018 peace agreement. The de facto border situation remains ambiguous in some sectors even after 2018.\n\n## 7. TIMOR-LESTE / EAST TIMOR (2002)\n\n- **Year:** 2002 (20 May — formal independence)\n- **Background:** Portugal's colony until 1975; invaded and annexed by Indonesia (1975–1999). After a UN-sponsored referendum (30 August 1999), 78.5% voted for independence. UN Transitional Administration (UNTAET) governed 1999–2002.\n- **Area:** ~14,874 km²\n- **Maritime boundary:** A long-running dispute with Australia over the Timor Sea was resolved by the 2018 Treaty on Maritime Boundaries, which gave Timor-Leste a significantly larger share of the sea (and the Greater Sunrise gas field revenue).\n\n## 8. SOUTH SUDAN INDEPENDENCE (2011)\n\n- **Year:** 2011 (9 July)\n- **Background:** The Comprehensive Peace Agreement (CPA) of 2005 ended the Second Sudanese Civil War and provided for a referendum. The January 2011 referendum returned 98.8% in favor of independence.\n- **Area:** ~619,745 km²\n- **Disputed areas:**\n  - **Abyei:** ~10,546 km². Status unresolved; a planned referendum has never been held. Administered jointly in theory, contested in practice.\n  - **Heglig/Panthou oil fields:** Brief South Sudanese military seizure in April 2012; returned to Sudan's control.\n  - Other border sectors (e.g., Kafia Kingi) remain contested.\n\n## 9. CRIMEA ANNEXATION BY RUSSIA (2014)\n\n- **Year:** 2014 (March)\n- **Nature:** Following the Ukrainian revolution of February 2014, Russian forces seized Crimea. A disputed referendum (16 March 2014) was held under military occupation, claiming 97% support for joining Russia. Russia formally annexed the Republic of Crimea and the federal city of Sevastopol on 18 March 2014.\n- **Area:** ~27,000 km² (Crimean peninsula including Sevastopol)\n- **International recognition:** The annexation is recognized by almost no state except Russia (and a few partial acknowledgments). UN General Assembly Resolution 68/262 (2014) affirmed Ukraine's territorial integrity (100 for, 11 against, 58 abstentions).\n\n## 10. RUSSIA-UKRAINE WAR TERRITORIAL CLAIMS (2022–PRESENT)\n\n- **Year:** 2022 (full-scale invasion 24 February 2022)\n- **Claimed annexations by Russia (30 September 2022):** Following sham referendums (23–27 September 2022), Russia claimed to annex four Ukrainian oblasts:\n\n| Oblast | Total area (km²) | Approx. Russian control (as of early 2025) |\n|---|---|---|\n| Donetsk | ~26,517 | ~60% |\n| Luhansk | ~26,684 | ~95% |\n| Zaporizhzhia | ~27,180 | ~70% (Russia does NOT hold Zaporizhzhia city) |\n| Kherson | ~28,461 | ~70% (Russia does NOT hold Kherson city; withdrew from right-bank Kherson in Nov 2022) |\n\n- **Total claimed (including Crimea):** ~136,000 km² — but Russia does not fully control any of the four oblasts it claims.\n- **International recognition:** Virtually no state recognizes these annexations. UN General Assembly Resolution ES-11/4 (October 2022) condemned the annexations 143–5.\n- **Status as of 2025:** Active conflict continues. Front lines shift. No peace agreement.\n\n## 11. HONG KONG AND MACAU HANDOVERS (1997, 1999)\n\n### Hong Kong\n- **Year:** 1997 (1 July)\n- **Nature:** Transfer from the United Kingdom to the People's Republic of China as a Special Administrative Region (SAR) under the \"one country, two systems\" framework (50-year guarantee, to 2047).\n- **Area:** ~1,106 km²\n- **Note:** This was NOT a change between two sovereign states in the usual sense — it was a scheduled handover under treaty (the 1984 Sino-British Joint Declaration). The 2020 National Security Law significantly curtailed Hong Kong's autonomy, but did not change its formal territorial status.\n\n### Macau\n- **Year:** 1999 (20 December)\n- **Nature:** Transfer from Portugal to PRC as a SAR.\n- **Area:** ~32.9 km² (and growing through land reclamation)\n\n## 12. WESTERN SAHARA\n\n- **Background:** Former Spanish Sahara, abandoned by Spain in 1975. Morocco and Mauritania partitioned the territory; Mauritania withdrew in 1979. The Polisario Front declared the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic (SADR) in 1976.\n- **Current situation:**\n  - Morocco controls ~80% of the territory (west of the Berm/sand wall), ~266,000 km² total for Western Sahara.\n  - SADR/Polisario controls a \"Free Zone\" east of the Berm (~20%), mostly empty desert.\n  - A 1991 ceasefire led to MINURSO deployment; a promised referendum has never been held.\n  - In November 2020, the ceasefire broke down and low-level hostilities resumed.\n  - In December 2020, the United States under Trump recognized Moroccan sovereignty over Western Sahara (linked to the Abraham Accords/Morocco-Israel normalization). This recognition has been maintained under subsequent administrations.\n  - The SADR is recognized by the African Union and ~40 states (though the number has been declining). Most Western states do not recognize either Morocco's sovereignty claim or the SADR.\n- **No definitive territorial resolution as of 2025.**\n\n## 13. ETHIOPIA-ERITREA BORDER\n\n- **Eritrea-Ethiopia War (1998–2000):** ~100,000 killed. Algiers Agreement (2000).\n- **EEBC ruling (2002):** Awarded Badme (~400 km² area) to Eritrea.\n- **Ethiopian refusal to implement** until PM Abiy Ahmed accepted the ruling in 2018 (for which he received the Nobel Peace Prize in 2019).\n- **Actual demarcation status:** Remains incomplete and ambiguous on the ground as of 2025.\n\n## 14. SUDAN-SOUTH SUDAN BORDER DISPUTES\n\n- See Section 8 above. Key unresolved areas:\n  - **Abyei** (~10,546 km²)\n  - **Kafia Kingi** (~30,000 km²) — administered by Sudan, claimed by South Sudan\n  - Multiple other \"14-Mile Area\" type disputes along the border.\n\n## 15. INDIA-BANGLADESH ENCLAVES EXCHANGE (2015)\n\n- **Year:** 2015 (effective 1 August 2015, pursuant to the Land Boundary Agreement ratified 7 May 2015)\n- **Nature:** India and Bangladesh exchanged 162 enclaves (including counter-enclaves and counter-counter-enclaves), resolving one of the world's most complex border situations dating to 1947/1713.\n  - India transferred 111 enclaves (~17,160 acres / ~69.4 km²) to Bangladesh.\n  - Bangladesh transferred 51 enclaves (~7,110 acres / ~28.8 km²) to India.\n- **Residents** were given the choice of which country to live in.\n- **Result:** Simplified, rationalized border.\n\n## 16. INDIA-PAKISTAN (KASHMIR)\n\n- **2019:** India revoked Article 370, revoking Jammu & Kashmir's special autonomous status, and bifurcated the former state into two Union Territories: Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh (31 October 2019).\n- **Nature:** This was an internal administrative change, not a border change, but it altered the governance structure of a disputed territory.\n- **Pakistan, China condemn** the move. The Line of Control (~740 km) and the Actual Ground Position Line (Siachen) remain unchanged.\n- In 2023, India's Supreme Court upheld the revocation.\n\n## 17. ISRAEL-PALESTINE AND GOLAN HEIGHTS\n\n- **2019:** United States recognized Israeli sovereignty over the Golan Heights (25 March 2019). The Golan Heights (~1,800 km²) were captured from Syria in 1967 and annexed by Israel in 1981 (not recognized by most states).\n- **2020:** Abraham Accords — normalization between Israel and UAE, Bahrain, Sudan, Morocco. No territorial changes per se (except the Western Sahara recognition linked to Morocco's accession).\n- **2023-2025:** Following the Hamas attack of 7 October 2023 and Israel's military campaign in Gaza, the territorial status of Gaza remains uncertain. Israel has conducted extensive military operations throughout Gaza (~365 km²). As of early 2025, Israel maintains military presence in parts of Gaza but the final political/territorial status remains entirely unresolved.\n- **West Bank settlements:** Continued expansion, but no formal border change recognized internationally.\n\n## 18. BOUGAINVILLE AUTONOMY REFERENDUM (2019)\n\n- **Year:** 2019 (23 November – 7 December)\n- **Nature:** Non-binding referendum in the Autonomous Region of Bougainville (Papua New Guinea). Result: 97.7% voted for independence.\n- **Area:** ~9,300 km²\n- **Status as of 2025:** The referendum result is not self-executing; it is subject to ratification by Papua New Guinea's national parliament. Negotiations continue. Independence has NOT been declared or recognized. A target date has been pushed beyond the original 2027 timeline.\n\n## 19. NEW CALEDONIA REFERENDUMS (2018–2021)\n\n- **Three referendums:**\n  - **2018 (4 November):** 56.7% voted to remain with France.\n  - **2020 (4 October):** 53.3% voted to remain with France.\n  - **2021 (12 December):** 96.5% voted to remain — but pro-independence groups boycotted, and turnout was only 43.9%.\n- **Area:** ~18,575 km²\n- **Status:** New Caledonia remains a French special collectivity (sui generis). Pro-independence groups (FLNKS) do not recognize the 2021 result. Civil unrest occurred in 2024 over electoral reform proposals. The political future remains contentious but no sovereignty change has occurred.\n\n## 20. SCOTLAND AND BREXIT\n\n### Scotland Referendum (2014)\n- **18 September 2014:** 55.3% voted No to independence. Scotland remains part of the United Kingdom. **No territorial change.**\n\n### Brexit (2016–2020)\n- **23 June 2016 referendum;** UK left the EU on 31 January 2020 (transition period ended 31 December 2020).\n- **No territorial border change.** However:\n  - The Northern Ireland Protocol (later the Windsor Framework, 2023) created a de facto customs/regulatory boundary in the Irish Sea between Great Britain and Northern Ireland.\n  - Gibraltar's relationship with the EU/Schengen is governed by a separate arrangement still under negotiation.\n  - The UK's sovereignty over its territory did not change.\n\n## 21. NAGORNO-KARABAKH / ARTSAKH (2020–2023)\n\n- **2020 (Second Nagorno-Karabakh War, 27 September – 10 November):**\n  - Azerbaijan recaptured the seven surrounding districts occupied by Armenian forces since the 1990s, plus the towns of Shusha/Shushi and Hadrut within the former NKAO.\n  - Ceasefire brokered by Russia (9/10 November 2020). Russian peacekeepers deployed along the Lachin corridor.\n  - Area recovered by Azerbaijan in 2020: ~5,000+ km² (the seven districts had a combined area of ~7,600 km²; plus parts of the former NKAO).\n\n- **2023 (19–20 September):**\n  - Azerbaijan launched a 24-hour military offensive against the remaining Armenian-controlled area of Nagorno-Karabakh.\n  - The self-declared Republic of Artsakh capitulated and announced it would dissolve itself effective 1 January 2024.\n  - Virtually the entire ethnic Armenian population (~100,000+) fled to Armenia.\n  - **Result:** The entire former Nagorno-Karabakh Autonomous Oblast (~4,400 km²) and all surrounding areas are under full Azerbaijani control. The \"Republic of Artsakh\" ceased to exist.\n\n## 22. RUSSIA-GEORGIA: SOUTH OSSETIA AND ABKHAZIA\n\n- **2008 (August):** Russo-Georgian War. Russia recognized South Ossetia (~3,900 km²) and Abkhazia (~8,660 km²) as independent states on 26 August 2008.\n- **Recognized by:** Russia, Nicaragua, Venezuela, Nauru, Syria (and previously by Vanuatu, Tuvalu — later withdrawn).\n- **\"Borderization\":** Russia has been progressively moving the de facto boundary fences/barbed wire deeper into Georgian-administered territory by hundreds of meters in some places (ongoing 2009–present).\n- **South Ossetia:** In 2022, the de facto leader announced a referendum on \"joining Russia,\" but this was subsequently postponed indefinitely.\n\n## 23. TURKEY-NORTHERN CYPRUS\n\n- **Northern Cyprus (TRNC):** Declared 1983, recognized only by Turkey. ~3,355 km². No change in status since 1991, but relevant as context.\n- **2004:** The Annan Plan referendum — Greek Cypriots voted No (75.8%), Turkish Cypriots voted Yes (64.9%). Reunification failed.\n- **No territorial change since 1974.**\n\n## 24. CHINA-BHUTAN BORDER AGREEMENT (2023–ONGOING)\n\n- China and Bhutan have been negotiating a boundary settlement. In October 2023, they signed a \"Cooperation Agreement on the Delimitation and Demarcation\" framework. The disputed areas total ~269 km² in Doklam/western sector and ~495 km² in northern sectors.\n- **No finalized border demarcation as of 2025.**\n\n## 25. MOROCCO-SPAIN (CEUTA, MELILLA, AND WESTERN SAHARA)\n\n- Ceuta (~18.5 km²) and Melilla (~12.3 km²) remain Spanish exclaves on the North African coast. Morocco periodically asserts claims. No territorial change.\n\n## 26. INDONESIA: WEST PAPUA\n\n- No sovereignty change. Low-level independence movement continues. West Papua remains part of Indonesia.\n\n## 27. SOMALILAND\n\n- **Declared independence from Somalia in 1991.** Area: ~176,120 km².\n- **Unrecognized** by any UN member state (as of 2025), though various states have engaged with it semi-officially.\n- **2024:** Ethiopia signed a memorandum of understanding with Somaliland for sea access / potential recognition, causing a diplomatic crisis with Somalia. Status remains unresolved.\n\n## 28. TAIWAN (REPUBLIC OF CHINA)\n\n- No territorial change since 1991. Taiwan's sovereignty status remains one of the world's most sensitive geopolitical issues. Recognized by a declining number of states (as of 2025, approximately 12 UN members). Cross-strait tensions have escalated since 2022 but no change in territorial control.\n\n## 29. FALKLAND ISLANDS / MALVINAS REFERENDUM (2013)\n\n- **2013:** 99.8% voted to remain a British Overseas Territory. Argentina disputes sovereignty. **No territorial change.** The war was in 1982.\n\n## 30. BORDER AGREEMENTS AND MINOR CHANGES\n\n### India-Bangladesh (Tin Bigha Corridor, 2011)\n- India began allowing 24-hour access through the Tin Bigha Corridor to the Bangladeshi enclave of Dahagram-Angarpota. Not a sovereignty transfer but a significant practical change.\n\n### Netherlands-Belgium (Baarle, 2016)\n- Minor border adjustments between the Netherlands and Belgium along the Meuse River (effective 2018), affecting a few hectares. Largely cosmetic rationalization.\n\n### Norway-Russia (2010)\n- Treaty on Maritime Delimitation in the Barents Sea, signed 2010, effective 7 July 2011. Divided a previously disputed maritime area of ~175,000 km² roughly equally.\n\n### Egypt-Saudi Arabia (Tiran and Sanafir Islands, 2017)\n- Egypt formally transferred sovereignty over the islands of Tiran (~80 km²) and Sanafir (~33 km²) in the Red Sea to Saudi Arabia in 2017 (ratified by Egyptian parliament, April 2017; formally handed over June 2022). Egypt had administered them since 1950.\n\n### Jordan-Saudi Arabia (2007)\n- A land swap was finalized in 2007: Jordan traded ~6,000 km² of inland desert for ~12 km of additional coastline on the Gulf of Aqaba.\n\n### Uzbekistan-Kyrgyzstan Border (2023–2024)\n- After decades of disputes, Uzbekistan and Kyrgyzstan completed a comprehensive border delimitation agreement in 2023, resolving the last contested segments of their ~1,378 km border, including enclaves.\n\n### Tajikistan-Kyrgyzstan (ongoing)\n- Violent clashes occurred in 2021 and 2022 over disputed border segments. Negotiations ongoing; about ~50% of the border was agreed as of 2024.\n\n### Azerbaijan-Armenia Border Delimitation (ongoing 2024–2025)\n- Following the 2020 and 2023 wars, negotiations on a peace treaty and border delimitation continue. Four villages in the Tavush/Gazakh sector were returned to Azerbaijan in 2024. A comprehensive agreement has not been reached.\n\n### China-Russia (2004-2008)\n- The 2004 Complementary Agreement resolved the last border disputes: Russia transferred Tarabarov Island and roughly half of Bolshoy Ussuriysky Island (Heixiazi Island) to China, effective 2008. Combined area: ~337 km².\n\n### Sudan-Egypt (Hala'ib Triangle)\n- ~20,580 km². Administered by Egypt, claimed by Sudan. No resolution. Egypt exercises de facto control.\n\n### Mauritius-Chagos Archipelago (2019–ongoing)\n- The International Court of Justice issued an advisory opinion in 2019 that the UK's separation of the Chagos Archipelago from Mauritius in 1968 was unlawful. UNGA voted 116–6 in May 2019 supporting Mauritius's claim.\n- In 2022, the UK announced negotiations to transfer sovereignty.\n- In October 2024, the UK and Mauritius reached an agreement in principle: sovereignty over the Chagos Archipelago (including Diego Garcia) would transfer to Mauritius, but the UK/US would retain a 99-year lease on Diego Garcia for the military base.\n- **Area:** ~60 km² of land, but a vast Exclusive Economic Zone.\n\n### France-Comoros (Mayotte)\n- Mayotte voted in 2009 to become a full French department (effective 2011). The Comoros claims Mayotte. **Area:** ~374 km².\n\n---\n\n## 31. SUMMARY TABLE OF ALL SOVEREIGNTY CHANGES (1990–2025)\n\n| Year | Event | Area (km²) | New/Changed Polities |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| 1990 | German reunification | 108,333 absorbed | GDR dissolved into FRG |\n| 1990 | Yemen unification | 528,000 combined | Republic of Yemen |\n| 1991 | USSR dissolution | ~22,402,200 | 15 successor states |\n| 1991 | Slovenia independence | 20,273 | Slovenia |\n| 1991 | Croatia independence | 56,594 | Croatia |\n| 1991 | Macedonia independence | 25,713 | North Macedonia |\n| 1991 | Somaliland declaration | 176,120 | Somaliland (unrecognized) |\n| 1992 | Bosnia independence | 51,197 | Bosnia & Herzegovina |\n| 1992 | FRY proclaimed | ~102,350 | Serbia + Montenegro |\n| 1993 | Czechoslovakia dissolution | 127,901 | Czech Republic + Slovakia |\n| 1993 | Eritrea independence | 117,600 | Eritrea |\n| 1997 | Hong Kong handover | 1,106 | Hong Kong SAR (PRC) |\n| 1999 | Macau handover | 32.9 | Macau SAR (PRC) |\n| 2002 | East Timor independence | 14,874 | Timor-Leste |\n| 2006 | Montenegro independence | 13,812 | Montenegro |\n| 2008 | Kosovo independence | 10,887 | Kosovo (partial recognition) |\n| 2008 | Russia recognizes S. Ossetia, Abkhazia | 12,560 | De facto states |\n| 2008 | China-Russia island transfer | 337 | Boundary adjustment |\n| 2011 | South Sudan independence | 619,745 | South Sudan |\n| 2014 | Crimea annexation by Russia | 27,000 | Disputed (Russia/Ukraine) |\n| 2015 | India-Bangladesh enclave exchange | ~98 total | Border rationalization |\n| 2017 | Tiran & Sanafir to Saudi Arabia | ~113 | Sovereignty transfer |\n| 2020 | Second Karabakh War | ~5,000+ | Azerbaijan recovers territory |\n| 2022 | Russia claims 4 Ukrainian oblasts | ~109,000 | Disputed (partially occupied) |\n| 2023 | Artsakh dissolved | ~4,400 | Azerbaijan regains full control |\n| 2024 | Chagos agreement (pending) | ~60 land | UK to Mauritius (in process) |\n\n---\n\n## 32. DATABASES AND SOURCES THAT TRACK HISTORICAL POLITIES AND BORDERS\n\n### A. Correlates of War (COW) Project\n- **URL:** https://correlatesofwar.org/\n- **Scope:** State system membership from 1816 to present. Codes sovereign states, start/end dates, territorial changes.\n- **Key datasets:** COW State System Membership (v2016 or later); Direct Contiguity; Territorial Change.\n- **Format:** CSV/tab-delimited.\n- **Strengths:** Long time depth, standardized coding, widely used in political science.\n- **Limitations:** Focuses on recognized sovereign states; does not include sub-state entities or de facto states in detail. Geographic data is coded but borders are not in GIS format.\n\n### B. CShapes / CShapes 2.0\n- **URL:** https://icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/\n- **Authors:** Nils Weidmann, Doreen Huber, Kristian Skrede Gleditsch.\n- **Scope:** GIS dataset of country boundaries, 1886–present (CShapes 2.0 extends to 2019).\n- **Format:** GeoJSON / Shapefile.\n- **Strengths:** Time-varying polygon geometries for every sovereign state and dependent territory. Links to COW and Gleditsch-Ward state codes. Monthly temporal resolution.\n- **Limitations:** Does not include de facto / unrecognized entities. Some border changes are slightly simplified. Needs updating past 2019.\n\n### C. Cliopatria\n- **URL:** https://cliopatria.io/ (project by various scholars)\n- **Scope:** Aims to map all polities in human history with territorial extents.\n- **Format:** Geospatial data.\n- **Strengths:** Uniquely ambitious scope covering pre-modern polities, empires, and historical states.\n- **Limitations:** Still in development; coverage uneven.\n\n### D. GADM (Database of Global Administrative Areas)\n- **URL:** https://gadm.org/\n- **Scope:** Current administrative boundaries for all countries (levels 0–5: country, state/province, district, etc.).\n- **Format:** Shapefile, GeoPackage, GeoJSON, RDS.\n- **Strengths:** Extremely detailed (sub-national levels). Free. Updated periodically.\n- **Limitations:** Only current boundaries — no historical time dimension.\n\n### E. Natural Earth\n- **URL:** https://www.naturalearthdata.com/\n- **Scope:** Public domain map data at 1:10m, 1:50m, 1:110m scales. Countries, disputed areas, breakaway regions.\n- **Format:** Shapefile, GeoPackage.\n- **Strengths:** Includes disputed territories and de facto boundaries. Clean cartographic data. Regularly updated.\n- **Limitations:** Current snapshot only; no historical time series.\n\n### F. Gleditsch-Ward (G-W) State System\n- **URL:** Part of Kristian Gleditsch's data page (https://ksgleditsch.com/)\n- **Scope:** A revised and extended version of the COW state list, with corrected dates and additions (e.g., micro-states). Covers 1816–present.\n- **Strengths:** Widely used in conjunction with CShapes. More inclusive than COW for small states.\n\n### G. Seshat: Global History Databank\n- **URL:** https://seshatdatabank.info/\n- **Scope:** Quantitative data on polities throughout human history (social complexity, warfare, etc.).\n- **Strengths:** Deep historical coverage. Includes territorial data.\n- **Limitations:** Focused more on sociopolitical variables than precise GIS boundaries.\n\n### H. Geopolitical Entities, Names, and Codes (GENC) / ISO 3166\n- **GENC:** US government standard (replaces FIPS). Updates with geopolitical changes.\n- **ISO 3166:** International standard for country codes. Updated by the ISO 3166 Maintenance Agency.\n- **Usefulness:** Code lists for tracking when polities appear/disappear (e.g., CS for Czechoslovakia, YU for Yugoslavia).\n\n### I. LSIB (Large Scale International Boundaries) — US State Department\n- **URL:** Available through the Humanitarian Data Exchange\n- **Scope:** The US State Department's authoritative dataset on international boundaries.\n- **Format:** Shapefile.\n- **Strengths:** Reflects US government positions on territorial disputes. Updated regularly.\n\n### J. World Bank / UN Geospatial\n- Various boundary datasets maintained by international organizations. The UN's \"Second Administrative Level Boundaries\" (SALB) dataset covers sub-national boundaries.\n\n### K. Wikidata / OpenStreetMap\n- **Wikidata:** Contains structured data on historical and current polities, including inception/dissolution dates, territory, and predecessor/successor relationships.\n- **OpenStreetMap:** Community-maintained vector data. Current boundaries only but very detailed.\n\n### L. V-Dem (Varieties of Democracy)\n- **URL:** https://www.v-dem.net/\n- **Scope:** Tracks political entities from 1789 to present. Not primarily a borders dataset, but codes polity existence and can be linked to geographic data.\n\n### M. GeaCron / Centennia Historical Atlas\n- **GeaCron (https://geacron.com/):** Interactive historical atlas covering 3000 BCE to present.\n- **Centennia:** Animated historical atlas focusing on Europe, 1000 CE to present.\n- **Limitations:** Commercial products; not raw data downloads.\n\n---\n\n## KEY OBSERVATIONS FOR POLITY-TRACKING PROJECTS\n\n1. **The most significant gap** in existing datasets is the period from 2019 to present. CShapes 2.0, the most widely used historical borders GIS dataset, ends in 2019 and does not capture the Nagorno-Karabakh changes, Russia's 2022 claimed annexations, or other recent events.\n\n2. **De facto vs. de jure** is a critical modeling decision. Datasets like COW and CShapes generally track only internationally recognized sovereign states. Entities like Somaliland, Transnistria, South Ossetia, Abkhazia, Northern Cyprus, and pre-2023 Artsakh need special treatment.\n\n3. **Partial recognition** (Kosovo, Palestine, Western Sahara/SADR, Taiwan) creates a spectrum that any polity database must handle with explicit coding rules.\n\n4. **Internal sovereignty changes** (e.g., India's revocation of Article 370 for Kashmir, Hong Kong's erosion of autonomy, federalization of Bosnia) matter for polity tracking even when international borders do not change.\n\n5. **Maritime boundaries** (Timor Sea, Barents Sea, South China Sea) represent a growing category of territorial change that land-focused datasets often omit.\n\n---\n\nThis survey covers all major sovereignty and territorial changes from 1990 through early 2025, along with the principal academic and geospatial data sources available for tracking them."}],"stop_reason":"end_turn","stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":3,"cache_creation_input_tokens":928,"cache_read_input_tokens":7571,"output_tokens":9253,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":928},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"}},"requestId":"req_011CZNX518vNi96D8TdneKbq","type":"assistant","uuid":"0b783f26-f1e4-494c-9c65-f6fc972d7b35","timestamp":"2026-03-24T20:18:40.013Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
